sentences.text: Good afternoon, Mayak
in my you if you can hear if you're on mute.
Can you hear me now?
Yes, we can, we can.
How's it going going?
Good, sir.
How about you doing?
Good, good to meet you.
I know it's been a kind of a long time coming for you and I, we've had a couple conversations throughout the year, but um how's the year been going so far?
So far?
So good.
Meaning obviously, it's, this year has been challenging for all different reasons.
You probably are in the multifamily uh real estate industry.
So uh aware of the extra supply coming into the market and getting some projections in terms of rent growth and all that.
But otherwise all good.
Uh The I think since we last spoke, I think our team has grown from at that time, we had like three individuals.
Now we have close to six individuals in my team.
OK.
Um So, so that has uh that has happened also over the course of time.
Now, your team to go in a little bit more context is sort of data analytics.
Um So my team covers um we are known as multi family operational strategy and analytics.
Uh So long term for the actual work is about revenue management.
Um So anything to do with uh pricing renewal pricing, new lease pricing.
Um And then thereafter, also into analytics, part of it, we are developing um the B I dashboard um based out of your network in Power B I with our IT team and a third party vendor.
Um Our team is responsible for budgets and forecasts.
Um And anything to do with uh utilities as well as expense management in general.
Well, ok, so a lot from the on the operational side using technology from utilities, expense management, revenue management, analytics.
Yeah, it's quite a lot.
Um What is and I know you've met with Justin here before.
Um It's probably been a while since you've seen our platform.
But I guess what is sort of your familiarity with Apartment IQ?
And um I'd love to hear more about your current market survey process if you don't mind sharing.
Yes.
So I think uh like when Justin had shared with me, um what I remember of it is an apartment IQ.
You're actually scraping the websites, the actual websites of your competitors and providing us a report which says what is the price point and how, what is their occupancy levels by unit type?
Because you have uh the number of units which are vacant displayed on the website.
So you can get a pulse of how many, what is their occupancy levels and what is their market rents?
Um And I think you can also share what is their concession levels or if they're giving one month free or two month free, anything of that kind.
Um And once you have that information, then there are different reports to benchmark about what our price points are in relation with the market um in relation to those specific concepts.
And the

our current market surveys are a little bit, uh I would say disjointed um disjointed in the sense that there are some uh areas or some communities which do participate in market surveys, like within their team members, they probably share uh reports like uh email thread which says what's your occupancy or what's your market trends like?
Um more often than not our um community managers visit the websites of our competitors and see what are the price points.
And then they put it in um in LRO which is our Revenue Management platform.
Um That doesn't mean it is, it's a one price point, it's not by, it's a unit type price point, but it is the lowest price point which is available.
It's not like they're taking an aggregate of all the units and all that, right?
So they look at whatever is one bedroom price and the lowest one if it is $1500 and then there is some concessions of one month free.
So they will put $1500.01 month free concession in LRO.
And that's how I know that what is the price point in the competitors?
And that's the market survey technique which we currently use.
OK.
That's awesome.
Yeah, there's a lot of context behind that process.
I appreciate you sharing.

Just with that in general, if you don't mind me asking.

in all transparency.
